name: Extensible Syntax
summary: Parsers that deliberately expose the grammar to user code at compile time; production examples are Racket readtables, Lean 4's runtime-extensible production table, and Rhombus/Shrubbery's two-phase reader+enforest design.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Extensible-syntax parsers expose the grammar itself as a user-mutable artifact. Where ordinary parsers fix the grammar at compile time (yacc, ANTLR) or expose grammar as ordered choice but no rebinding (PEG), extensible-syntax languages let user code change parse behavior mid-file: redefine character meanings (Racket readtables), declare new grammar productions (Lean 4 `syntax`/`macro_rules`), or compose nested grammars (Rhombus's reader + enforest).

The shared thesis: language extension should be a user-level library concern, not a privileged compiler-authoring one. The cost is a parser that must accommodate a runtime-changing grammar, which rules out most table-driven generators.

sections.title: Racket — readtables and #lang
sections.content:
Racket's reader is the oldest of the family. `read-syntax` walks input character by character consulting a **readtable**: a first-class, mutable mapping from characters to reader procedures. Any character can be rebound to start a new syntactic form. `#lang` goes further, dispatching to an arbitrary user-defined reader module that can implement a whole non-s-expression surface syntax — and the result is always a syntax object carrying source locations and hygienic lexical context, suitable for macro expansion downstream.

This is the canonical "the reader is a library, not a table of productions" design. Racket has used it for decades to ship languages on the Racket runtime: Typed Racket, Pollen, Scribble, the Beautiful Racket teaching languages, and many domain-specific notations.

sections.title: Lean 4 — runtime-extensible production tables
sections.content:
Lean 4 (de Moura & Ullrich, CADE 2021) takes the idea into a dependently typed prover: a lexerless, memoising combinator-style recursive descent reads a runtime-extensible production table that users grow with `syntax` and `macro_rules` forms mid-file, with per-category Pratt precedence and built-in macro hygiene.

The grammar is a first-class compiler artifact: `syntax (priority := high) "!" term : term` adds a new prefix operator at parse time, and `macro_rules` declares how the new syntax is rewritten into existing syntax. Because the parser is a recursive-descent combinator over the production table, adding entries is a constant-time runtime operation rather than a regenerate-and-rebuild step. This is the same design space as Racket's readtables but at a coarser granularity (production rules rather than per-character readers).

sections.title: Rhombus / Shrubbery — two-phase parens-free
sections.content:
**Rhombus / Shrubbery** (Flatt et al., OOPSLA 2023) is Racket's parens-free variant. Parsing splits in two: a **shrubbery** reader produces an indentation-and-bracket tree with no knowledge of operators or precedence, and a second **enforest** pass (a Honu-style refinement) resolves operators and binding forms through user-definable macros.

This decouples the lexical/structural shape (handled by shrubbery, fixed) from the operator/binder shape (handled by enforest, extensible). The shrubbery layer means that even before any macros are defined, the source has consistent block structure; the enforest layer means that operators and forms are user-extensible without violating the shrubbery's structural assumptions.

sections.title: Design tradeoffs
sections.content:
Extensible-syntax languages buy first-class language extension at the cost of a parser that cannot be table-driven, conflict-detection that becomes a runtime concern (or absent), and tooling (language servers, formatters) that must replay the user's grammar extensions to even produce a parse tree. The Racket ecosystem solves this by making the syntax object the canonical artifact and shipping language extensions as libraries that the editor loads alongside user code.

Projectional editors take a different tack: instead of letting users redefine the grammar, the editor manipulates AST nodes directly so that grammar ambiguity is avoided by construction. **JetBrains MPS** (Meta Programming System) is the longest-running production projectional editor — programmers edit a structured projection of the AST rather than text, and language composition is automatic because there is no concrete-syntax grammar to compose.

For a language that doesn't need user-defined syntax, the simpler choice (fixed grammar) avoids these costs. For a language whose value proposition includes user-extensible notation — DSLs, theorem provers, domain-specific languages embedded in source — extensible syntax is the price of admission.
